narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 94% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in typical successful applicants. This high predicted failure risk directly led to the rejection of your application, as the school uses this tool to prioritize students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which placed you in the 2nd decile—well below the average of around the 5th or 6th for those who pass. This low Year 3 standing was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 28, significantly under the typical 37 seen in passers, which further tilted the odds against you by highlighting gaps in standardized legal reasoning skills. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average, reinforced this pattern, contributing notably to the failure prediction despite your decent undergrad grades. Compared to typical students who pass—those with higher deciles, LSATs around 37, and stronger early academic momentum—your profile showed consistent underperformance in key school metrics that the model weighs heavily for bar success. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the norm, they weren't enough to offset these core concerns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted coursework or tutoring, and reapplying with evidence of improvement, such as recent high-stakes achievements. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas, and admissions teams value demonstrated growth. Your background positions you well for this—keep pushing, as persistence often turns predictions around.
